name: Pozo de Pozuelo de Aragón -
name_original: Pozo de Pozuelo de Aragón - Limpiaque — circular desde La Almudia de Doña Godina
description: Cycle a moderate 49.5-mile (79.7 km) route near La Almunia de Doña Godina, featuring the unique Pozo de Pozuelo de Aragón geyser.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around La Almunia De Doña Godina traverse a varied landscape at the foothills of the Iberian System, particularly the Sierra de Algairén. The region features a mix of agricultural lands with fruit orchards and riverbanks along the Mediano, Grío, and Jalón rivers, alongside more challenging terrain with significant elevation gains. Cyclists encounter mostly paved surfaces, with some routes extending into areas with canyons and narrow passages. This diverse topography provides options for both moderate rides and demanding climbs. Best road cycling routes around La Almunia De Doña Godina The most popular road cycling route is Aranda Comarca Loop, a 50.5 miles (81.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 53 minutes to complete. This difficult route features over 3,600 feet of elevation gain through varied terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Alto de Codos Loop from Cariñena, a difficult 58.7 miles (94.5 km) path. This route includes challenging climbs with over 5,300 feet of elevation, offering extensive views of the surrounding area. Local road cyclists also love the Alfamén and Santuario Virgen de las Lagunas — circular from La Almunia de Doña Godina, a 23.4 miles (37.6 km) trail leading through agricultural landscapes and past cultural sites, often completed in about 1 hour 37 minutes. Road cycling around La Almunia De Doña Godina is defined by the foothills of the Sierra de Algairén, fertile agricultural lands, and river valleys. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in La Almunia De Doña Godina are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.75 stars from more than 5 reviews. More than 1,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore La Almunia De Doña Godina's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in La Almunia de Doña Godina?
A: There are over 60 road cycling routes around La Almunia de Doña Godina, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels. You'll find approximately 9 easy routes, 16 moderate routes, and 39 challenging routes to explore.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around La Almunia de Doña Godina?
A: The terrain varies significantly, from the foothills of the Sierra de Algairén to fertile agricultural lands and river valleys. You'll primarily encounter paved surfaces, but some longer or more challenging routes may include unpaved segments. The region offers everything from gentle rides through fruit orchards to demanding climbs with substantial elevation gains.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easy ride?
A: Yes, there are several easy road cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991362586/alfamen-and-santuario-virgen-de-las-lagunas-circular-from-la-almunia-de-dona-godina" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alfamén and Santuario Virgen de las Lagunas — circular from La Almunia de Doña Godina&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 23.4-mile (37.6 km) trail that takes you through agricultural landscapes and past cultural sites with minimal elevation.

Q: Where can I find challenging road cycling routes with significant elevation gains?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988409048/alto-de-codos-loop-from-carinena" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Codos Loop from Cariñena&lt;/a&gt; offer extensive climbs with over 5,300 feet of elevation gain over 58.7 miles (94.5 km). Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20145989" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Codos – ⭐️ Puerta de Terrer loop from Cariñena&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 136 km with over 1800 meters of ascent.

Q: What natural features and scenic viewpoints can I discover while road cycling in the region?
A: You can enjoy diverse natural beauty, from the lush fruit orchards and riverbanks along the Mediano, Grío, and Jalón rivers to the impressive canyons and narrow passages near Ricla, Morata, and Chodes. Notable viewpoints include the Hermitage of the Virgin of the Eagle, offering magnificent views of the Sierra de Algairén and Moncayo. You might also encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5288150" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Muel Waterfalls&lt;/a&gt; or the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5271458" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valcongosto Gorge&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the region is rich in history. You can encounter sites like the Romanesque chapel of Nuestra Señora de Cabañas with its Gothic mural paintings, medieval architecture in the town's stately homes, and the Mudejar tower. The Hermitage of the Virgin of the Eagle, located atop Pico del Águila, is another significant cultural and historical point of interest.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in La Almunia de Doña Godina?
A: The region offers a scenic cycling experience throughout the year. Spring brings fields in bloom and fruit trees, while autumn provides vibrant colors. The climate is generally favorable, but it's always advisable to check seasonal weather conditions, especially for longer rides or higher elevations in the Sierra de Algairén.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989952691/aranda-comarca-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aranda Comarca Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 50.5-mile (81.2 km) route, or the easier &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991362586/alfamen-and-santuario-virgen-de-las-lagunas-circular-from-la-almunia-de-dona-godina" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alfamén and Santuario Virgen de las Lagunas — circular from La Almunia de Doña Godina&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in La Almunia de Doña Godina?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.75 stars from more than 5 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied landscape, which combines challenging mountain foothills with serene agricultural paths, and the well-maintained paved surfaces that make for an enjoyable ride.

Q: Are there any mountain passes accessible by road bike in the area?
A: Yes, the region offers several mountain passes that are popular with road cyclists. You can find passes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5302628" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collado de la Luz&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5302624" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collado del Tío Francisco&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4023442" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Codos&lt;/a&gt;, which provide both challenging climbs and rewarding views.

Q: Can I find routes that pass by rivers or water features?
A: Absolutely. The region is traversed by rivers such as the Mediano, Grío, and Jalón. Many routes follow riverbanks lined with poplars and alders, offering lush and scenic sections. The Muel Natural Park, with its waterfalls and the well-preserved Roman Dam of Muel, is another area where you can enjoy water features.
tips: At this junction we can choose to turn off the dirt road to the Pozuelo de Aragón Geyser, a 325-meter-deep well and an impressive spout of water as if it were a geyser. We will find an information panel that will tell us every detail of the place.

As we pass through the beautiful town of Lumpiaque belonging to the Valdejalón region, we can visit the parish church of San Francisco de Asís, built in the 16th century with a beautiful Mudejar bell tower, currently listed as a World Heritage Site; or also the Hermitage of Santa Bárbara, dating from the 17th century.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular road cycling route is approximately 79.7 kilometers long and typically takes about 3 hours and 14 minutes to complete. This duration is an estimate and can vary based on your cycling speed and stops along the way.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is classified as a moderate road cycling experience, generally featuring mostly paved surfaces. While some segments might be unpaved or challenging to ride in the broader region, this specific route primarily offers a smooth ride suitable for road bikes.

faqs.question: What level of fitness is required for this route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's moderate difficulty and an elevation gain of around 420 meters, cyclists should possess a good level of fitness. While moderate routes are often suitable for various fitness levels, the elevation gain suggests it's not entirely flat and will require some effort.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest on the route?
faqs.answer: A key highlight is the unique Geyser of Pozuelo de Aragón, an artesian well with thermal and saline water spouting 2-3 meters high. You'll also pass by the Parish Church of San Francisco de Asís of Lumpiaque, offering a glimpse into local architecture and history.

faqs.question: Where does the route start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The route is a circular one starting and ending from La Almunia de Doña Godina. You should be able to find parking within the town to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Aragón region generally offers good cycling conditions during spring and autumn, with milder temperatures. Summer can be quite hot, so early mornings or late afternoons are preferable if cycling during that season. Winter can be cooler, but often still suitable for cycling on clear days.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this public road cycling route.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As this is a road cycling route, bringing dogs along on the bike itself is generally at your discretion and depends on your setup (e.g., a bike trailer). However, dogs are not typically expected to run alongside cyclists on a route of this distance and type due to traffic and safety concerns.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or get refreshments along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several towns, including Pozuelo de Aragón and Lumpiaque, where you can expect to find local cafes, bars, or shops to refuel and get refreshments.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of the Camino de los Corrales, Acceso a la Cueva del Gato, and Plaza de España, though these cover only a very small percentage of the overall journey.

faqs.question: What should I wear or bring for cycling this route?
faqs.answer: For this moderate road cycling route, wear appropriate cycling attire, including a helmet, padded shorts, and layers suitable for the weather. Bring plenty of water, snacks, a basic repair kit (spare tube, pump), and your phone for navigation and emergencies.

waypoints.name: Parish Church of San Francisco de Asís of Lumpiaque
